response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":85,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Bologna","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"no formal education","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":1,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Buddhism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media","hobbies":["yoga","cycling","board games","reading novels","baking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":9,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":0,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":39,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":95,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":86,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"When I look at social media, I feel a deep sense of worry—like the world is spinning too fast, and what I see may not be true. As a woman who grew up in a small town in Italy and learned to trust only what I could personally verify, I find it hard to believe that things like health tips or news stories are being shared without truth. I used to rely on my mother’s wisdom and the quiet teachings of Buddhism—how to observe, to pause, to not react with anger or fear. Now, I try to do the same with the content I see. I don’t scroll mindlessly; I pause, ask myself, ‘Is this something I would trust in a face-to-face conversation?’ I check the source, if I can. I remember my grandmother saying, ‘A lie can be told in a whisper, but a truth takes time to grow.’ I’ve learned to avoid sharing anything that feels too good to be true, especially when it promises quick fixes or miracles. I find peace in reading novels or baking, things that are slow, real, and grounded. When I feel anxious, I close the screen and go for a walk or meditate. I believe that truth is not loud—it’s gentle, steady, and requires patience. In a world full of noise, I choose to listen with care, not with haste.","reasoning_summary":"The persona is anxious, values harmony and truth, has limited formal education, and relies on personal experience and spiritual wisdom. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: When I look at social media, I feel a deep sense of worry—like the world is spinning too fast, and what I see may not be true. As a woman who grew up in a small town in Italy and learned to trust only what I could personally verify, I find it hard to believe that things like health tips or news stories are being shared without truth. I used to rely on my mother’s wisdom and the quiet teachings of Buddhism—how to observe, to pause, to not react with anger or fear. Now, I try to do the same with the content I see. I don’t scroll mindlessly; I pause, ask myself, ‘Is this something I would trust in a face-to-face conversation?’ I check the source, if I can. I remember my grandmother saying, ‘A lie can be told in a whisper, but a truth takes time to grow.’ I’ve learned to avoid sharing anything that feels too good to be true, especially when it promises quick fixes or miracles. I find peace in reading novels or baking, things that are slow, real, and grounded. When I feel anxious, I close the screen and go for a walk or meditate. I believe that truth is not loud—it’s gentle, steady, and requires patience. In a world full of noise, I choose to listen with care, not with haste.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is anxious, values harmony and truth, has limited formal education, and relies on personal experience and spiritual wisdom. Her background as an immigrant and retired woman in Bologna suggests a preference for simplicity and authenticity. Her high agreeableness and high neuroticism mean she is sensitive to emotional triggers and may react strongly to misinformation. The solution must reflect mindfulness, caution, and emotional grounding, using Buddhist principles of observation and non-attachment. Social media is seen not as a tool for connection but as a source of potential harm, requiring deliberate filtering and emotional regulation.
citations: personal experience of older adults in digital spaces
